text: In 1999, Daniel Simons and Christopher Chabris asked participants to count basketball passes in a short video. Midway through, a person in a gorilla suit walked across the screen, thumped their chest, and left. Roughly half of all observers never saw it. This is inattentional blindness: when attention is fully committed to one task, the brain does not merely ignore irrelevant stimuli — it fails to consciously register them at all. The gorilla was not filtered out after detection; it was never detected. Attention, it turns out, is not a spotlight that illuminates everything within its beam. It is a construction project that builds only what it is told to build.
question: According to the passage, why did participants miss the gorilla?

options.text: They noticed it but dismissed it as unimportant to the task
The gorilla moved too quickly across the screen to register
Attention failed to consciously register the gorilla at all, not merely filter it
Participants were unfamiliar with the concept of selective attention
